text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'll discuss how you can improve your AI apps with automated eval. I'll start by describing what eval are and why they are important and then walk through a concrete example where I used an eval driven approach to systematically improve a LinkedIn ghost writer. The thing that makes LLM so powerful is their ability to perform arbitrary tasks from just natural language. We could give it a popular book and ask it to summarize it and it would come up with a nice concise summary for us. Then in the very next moment we can say actually I want you to write a new chapter to this book and then we'll be able to do that as well. Or you can do something a bit more technical and sophisticated and ask the model to analyze all the literary devices used in chapter 3 and visualize them in a plot. And this typically works pretty well because if we're just having the LLM do ad hoc tasks through an interface like chatgbt or claude, you can always manually review whatever output it spits out and make sure it is error-free or good enough for your specific use case. But this approach does have its limitations and that's simply because human evals don't scale. So for example, if instead of just doing one chapter, we wanted the LLM to analyze all the literary devices in all of the chapters and visualize them in plots, then this is going to take significantly more work from us in the manual review process. But okay, maybe I have to review a dozen plots. This isn't such a big deal. But what if this went up in order of magnitude and there were like over a hundred plots that you had to review? So whatever your tolerance for tedious verification work might be, there is going to be some limit where human evaluation is no longer viable. In these cases, the natural thought is, well, we have these powerful LLMs and we have powerful computers. Can't we just automate these evaluations? That's exactly the idea with automated emails, which is getting computers to evaluate LLM responses. So now we can do the same task of analyze all the literary devices and visualize them in a plot. But now instead of having a human manually review and verify this, we can just have an LLM judge verify the output to the LLM. There's really no difference in evaluating one of these outputs or evaluating a hundred or a thousand of these outputs. This scalability is very important because automating these evaluations unlock the systematic improvement of LLM applications. For example, the typical way of improving an LLM application. So let's say we want to improve it through prompt engineering. The typical workflow will look like this. We'll generate a prompt. We'll give it to the LLM. We'll have it generate an output and then we'll manually review and verify the output. And then based on our evaluation, we'll actually go back and refine the prompt to correct for some subtle mistakes. And then we'll give it back to the LM, spit out another response, and then we'll do another evaluation. We'll see that there are some other issues that came up. And then we'll update the prompt again, give it to the LM, generate an output, evaluate the output again, and just continue in this loop until we have a prompt that is pretty good. By automating the evaluation process, we go from this manual workflow to automating this whole process. What this unlocks is with the manual evals, you know, maybe you'll look at tens or hundreds of examples. But if you're automating this evaluation process, there's really no cap on the number of times that you can go through this loop. And even if you do have some kind of budget constraints or time constraints, you're going to be able to go through this loop much faster if a computer is doing the eval versus a person. What this means is if you're only looking at 10 or 100 examples and refining your prompt, you're only going to see errors that tend to come up one to 10% of the time. So if you're building an application and then deploying it to thousands of users, they're going to be errors that come up in production that you just never would have seen in development because you're not looking at enough examples. However, if you've automated this process, you can see those errors that only come up one in a thousand times or one in 10,000 times. These errors that only come up when you scale your system. Now that we know what automated evals are and why we should care about them, how do we actually create these things? Just to recap, an eval is simply a number that correlates with a good or bad LLM response. Now, I actually talked all about this in a previous video on evals, but here I'll just do a quick recap on the two ways we can automate evals. So, the first is we can have codebased evals. This is things like computing the length of an LLM's response, counting the number of times a specific word or string occurs in a response, checking the formatting, seeing if the response is in markdown or contains some XML tag or is proper SQL code, and on and on and on. There are endless code-based evals that we can implement and the specific ones that you create are going to be specific to your use case. The other way we can automate evals is using LLMs. And in the previous video I talked about two key categories for LLM based evals. There are global evals. So basically assessing if a response is right or wrong, good or bad. Or we can have relative evaluations. Basically, the LLM just tells you if one response is better or worse than another. Each of these types of evals has its pros and cons. The pros of code-based evals is that they are relatively easy to implement and they're easy to interpret because if your evaluation is simply checking if the LLM outputed Python code that actually runs, that's a very black and white, very easy to interpret evaluation. However, the downside of code-based evals is that they may not track with your desired result. For example, if you want to evaluate the empathy of a customer support chatbot, then that's going to be something that's pretty hard to implement using some kind of codebased eval. And so, you'd probably need to turn to something like an LLM based eval. The number one pro of using LLMs to do eval is that you can basically evaluate anything. You can evaluate things like empathy, how well the response captures a specific style, the quality of a summarization, and so on and so forth. However, the downsides of LLM based eval include they are difficult to implement. As I'll talk about later in this video, building your LLM judge can be just as much work, if not even more work, than building your main AI system. And then the second thing is that LLM based eval are never going to be perfect. They're going to have some empirical accuracy that you're going to have to take into account when you're using it to grade outputs from your main AI system. So to make this all more concrete, I want to walk through a specific example here. I'll talk about an eval driven LinkedIn ghost writer. Basically, I want to give the LLM raw notes of LinkedIn post ideas and then I want it to output LinkedIn posts in my unique style and voice. In the previous video, we saw how I was improving this system using the manual feedback loop. So, basically, I would generate outputs from the LLM. I would manually review the outputs, evaluate them, and then return to the prompt, refine it, and then continue in this feedback loop. The first step builds on top of the work I discussed in the previous video where I used error analysis to identify common failure modes of this LinkedIn ghostriter. The second step is to create evals for each of the failure modes. So these could be codebased or LLM based. The third step is to curate a development and test set of user inputs. So these are realistic inputs that we're going to pass into this LinkedIn ghostriter. Then the fourth step is to generate posts from the user inputs in the dev set using the prompt of the LinkedIn ghost writer. And then we will apply the evals we created in step two to the posts that we just generated. And then we will review the results and refine the prompt. In other words, we can look at all the outputs that failed our evaluations and then we can use those failures to inform improvements to our prompt. So in other words, does it sound like me or does it not sound like me? Once we have this labeled data set of posts, we can split it into a train test and development set. We'll have a development set. So this is what we're going to use to refine our prompt. We'll have a test set which is going to be a held out set of data that we never look at which we can use to objectively evaluate our LLM judge. And then we can have a train set which includes examples we can incorporate in the LLM judges prompt. The next step is to create an initial evaluation prompt. This is going to be some basic prompt engineering and we can incorporate these training examples in this LLM judge prompt. Next, we can pass the dev set through the LLM judge. So, we'll take the posts from the development set. We will pass it to the LLM with our LLM judge prompt. And the output of this is going to be a label for each of the posts. This is going to be our judges prediction of whether the post sounded like me or didn't sound like me. So, now that we have the judges label, we can compare these to the ground truth. And so for each of our posts in the dev set, we have a ground truth label of whether it sounded like me or didn't sound like me. And then we have what the LLM judge thought sounded like me and didn't sound like me. And so we can just compare the LLM judge's predictions to the ground truth. And then finally, we can review the errors and refine the prompt. We can look at the posts where the judge's prediction didn't match the ground truth. So, one thing I did a lot was what people might call metaraprompting, where I basically gave the LLM judge one of these posts that it got wrong, told it that it got the label wrong, and then asked it to suggest improvements to the prompt to avoid that mistake in the future. This was the main strategy I used to refine the prompt. It wasn't very often that I was coming up with improvements.
